schools.summary: The 'long war' was a state of hostility, not pitched battle; time steadily transferred strength to David as Saul's house declined.
Gill fixes the chronology: five further years of skirmishing until Ishbosheth's death made David king over all Israel.
Henry: God prolonged the war to try David's faith; the decline of Saul's house pictures grace overcoming corruption in the believer.
Wesley: the war ran five years more, Ishbosheth being crowned soon after Saul's death.
Keil: the list of Hebron sons is deliberate proof of the strengthening house of David, following the custom of opening a reign with family notices.
Gill: David had wives in exile but no sons until Hebron, so this fruitfulness marks the blessing on his reign there.
Gill: the second son is Daniel ('God hath judged me' against Nabal) and Chileab ('like his father'), so like David in his features.
Keil records the pedigree of Absalom, son of Maacah daughter of Talmai king of Geshur, within the Hebron list.
Wesley: David married the Geshurite princess in policy for a northern ally, but 'paid dear' for it in Absalom's later rebellion.
Gill: the fourth son Adonijah is the future usurper who anticipated Solomon and died by his order, so the birth-notice foreshadows a later crisis.
Gill weighs why only Eglah is called 'David's wife' — either an obscure woman or, as the Jews say, Michal ('heifer') — and rejects the Michal view on chronological grounds.
Wesley canvasses the same two readings undecided, and turns the tally of six sons by six wives into a warning against David's multiplied marriages.

debates.question: Why is only Eglah called 'David's wife,' and is she Michal?

sides.position: an obscure woman marked only by her relation to David
Michal, his first and best-loved wife ('Eglah' = heifer)
holders: some Jewish doctors (per Gill and Wesley)

schools.summary: Abner's firm support alone held up Saul's house; Keil and Ellicott read the verse as the setup for the coming quarrel that will topple it.
Gill: Abner strengthened Saul's house only until the quarrel with Ishbosheth broke it.
Wesley notes Abner's zeal to explain his later resentment; Clarke reads his self-strengthening and the concubine as a bid for the throne.

debates.question: Did taking Rizpah signal Abner's design on the kingdom?

sides.position: yes — an evident proof he wished to seize the government
probably not — he was still upholding Ishbosheth

themes: Abner the prop of Saul's house
a king dependent on his captain

schools.summary: Taking a royal concubine asserted a claim to the throne; the implied charge of treason, not mere immorality, is what enrages Abner.
Henry: Ishbosheth's rash accusation was politically foolish, yet God serves his purposes by the follies of men, turning it to David's advantage.
Gill: Abner had reason for wrath whether the charge was false, or true and (as he thought) a small fault in one who had served so well.
Clarke: Abner, 'the only prop of thy tottering kingdom,' resents being treated with the indignity of a dog's head.
Ellicott and Keil correct the phrase to 'a dog's head belonging to Judah' — Abner protesting he is treated as a contemptible traitor despite his service to Saul's house.
Gill: Abner never denies the deed but reckons it a trifle, indignant to be reproved by one he had so served.
No explicit divine oath is on record, but God's unalterable promise (1 Sam 15:29) has an oath's force; Ellicott exposes Abner acting from revenge, not to fulfil God's will.
Clarke: since Abner knew God had given David the kingdom, his long resistance meant he had been consciously fighting against God.
Gill: Abner insolently takes to himself what belongs to the Lord, as if the kingdom could not be transferred without him.

debates.question: What oath of the Lord to David does Abner mean?

sides.position: the unalterable divine promise/choice, equivalent to an oath though not verbally recorded

schools.summary: 'From Dan to Beersheba' marks the transfer of the whole nation; Ellicott sees Abner's ready resolve as proof he had already contemplated defection, giving ground to Ishbosheth's reproach.
Gill: Abner insolently claims for himself the transfer of the kingdom that belonged to the Lord to accomplish.
Gill: Ishbosheth had matter to answer but 'durst not' say it — fear, not lack of argument, silenced him.
Keil: he could not reply 'because he was afraid of him' — a king in terror of his own general.
Clarke: miserable the king ruled by his general; military men should never hold civil power.
Henry: conscious of his weakness Ishbosheth kept silence, become 'a bowing wall and a tottering fence' (Ps. 62:3).
'Whose is the land?' is read two ways; Ellicott judges Abner means 'who but I can deliver it?', fitting his pride, and explains the repeated 'saying' as two parts of the message.
Gill: Abner treats on his own account, secretly and unknown to Ishbosheth, for his own advantage.
Wesley: God overrules the passions of wicked men to his own ends; the land is David's 'by Divine right.'

debates.question: What does Abner mean by 'Whose is the land?'

sides.position: 'Is it not thine by promise / Divine right?'
'Who but I have power to deliver it to thee?'

schools.summary: David's demand for Michal rests on justice (she was wrongly taken), affection, and politics — showing no malice to Saul's house and strengthening his own claim.
Clarke: Michal alone had full legitimacy; prudence forbade leaving a princess with a man who might press a claim through her, and she was David's lawful wife.
Gill: David readily accepts the league and forgives past offences, on this single condition.
David demands Michal from Ishbosheth himself so the restoration is legal and public — not a seizure by force — resting his claim on the bride-price he paid.
Gill: Abner could not deliver her himself, so advised David to apply to Ishbosheth, her brother, and would then use his interest to secure it.
Wesley: Ishbosheth's consent was needed; the demand offered him hope of reconciliation, and David sought Michal from affection and to strengthen his title.
Keil: Ishbosheth (through Abner) fetched Michal from Gallim; 'the husband was obliged to consent' — Phaltiel had no standing to refuse.
Gill: Michal is taken from her second husband, to whom Saul had given her when he forced her from David.
Keil traces Phaltiel's grieving walk to Bahurim, the border of Judah; Ellicott grants his sincere affection but insists Michal was not rightfully his and David's claim was prior and better.
Gill: Phaltiel wept from great affection, unwilling but forced to part with her at the king's command.
Clarke calls it 'a pity' to take her from a devoted husband, yet grants David's legal right, since she was never divorced but taken by violence.

debates.question: How much sympathy does the weeping Phaltiel deserve?

sides.position: real pathos — a devoted husband wronged
genuine grief, but Michal was not rightfully his and David's claim is prior

themes: Bahurim and the border of Judah

schools.summary: Abner reminds the elders they had long desired David as king; Keil and Ellicott note the popular voice already favoured David, and that Abner himself had been the obstacle to his recognition.
Gill: Abner conferred with the tribal chiefs, using his influence to turn them toward David.

Abner cites God's promise to save Israel 'by the hand of David'; since no such saying is recorded verbatim, Ellicott suggests an unrecorded prophetic word or a fair inference, and Keil that its substance was widely known.
Clarke: 'such a promise is not extant,' so it means only 'thus, it may be presumed, God hath determined.'
Gill: Abner's 'Now then do it' carries his own confession that he had until now opposed David's God-appointed reign.

debates.question: Where did the Lord 'speak of David' as Abner claims?

sides.position: an unrecorded prophetic utterance or the settled sense of David's preserved history
no such promise is extant — it means 'it may be presumed God hath determined'

schools.summary: Benjamin needed special negotiation, bound to Saul's house by kinship and privilege; Abner, himself a Benjamite, then reported all to David at Hebron.
Gill: Abner had greatest influence in his own tribe, whose nearness to Judah and loyalty to Saul made winning it essential.
Wesley: he applied himself to Benjamin as most loyal to Saul, most loath to lose the crown, and a valiant tribe bordering Judah.

The twenty men are representatives of Israel confirming Abner's report; the feast is a solemn sacrificial covenant-meal, and 'he went in peace' is carefully worded to foreshadow the treachery to come.
Gill: Abner, having won many to David, now comes openly and publicly with the nation's principal men to seal the league.
Gill: Abner undertakes to travel through all Israel and gather the tribes so David may reign over all his heart desires.
Clarke: 'he went in peace' shows David dismissed Abner in good faith, with no sinister design against him.
Joab returns from a raid with great spoil to find Abner already gone; the historian repeats that Abner left in peace under David's safe conduct.
Gill (with Wesley): the 'troop' was robbers exploiting the civil war, whom Joab overtook and despoiled.
Henry: Joab had every reason to trust David's wise dealing, yet returned to chide his king — the collision that will end in Abner's blood.
Keil: told that Abner had come and gone in peace, Joab resolves to reproach David and to paint Abner a spy, masking his private revenge.
Gill: the chief officers reported to David at court that Abner had come and been sent away in peace.
Joab's 'What hast thou done?' slanders Abner as a spy; Ellicott finds honest suspicion mixed with fear of being supplanted and desire to avenge Asahel, all aimed at poisoning David's mind.
Henry: astonished at Joab's effrontery in reproaching his king to his face; David answered nothing, not from fear but from contempt.
Gill: Joab came to the king in a passion at the news of Abner's peaceful dismissal.

Gill: Joab's 'Thou knowest Abner' may be assertion or a stinging question; he exploits Abner's known cunning to make the charge of espionage seem self-evident against a man who had in fact come in peace.
Joab recalls Abner from the well of Sirah, near Hebron, in David's name but without his knowledge; Abner's unsuspecting return proves his entire confidence in David.
Gill: Joab flew out in a passion without awaiting the king's answer, showing how great his power over David had grown.
Joab lures Abner aside in the gate and stabs him under the fifth rib 'for the blood of Asahel'; Ellicott and Keil note the pretext of private conference and revenge quickened by jealousy.
Clarke: fearing Abner would be made captain, Joab murdered him under pretense of avenging Asahel — 'a cool-blooded, finished murderer' the king dared not punish.
Henry: God righteously set aside the bad man Abner, but Joab wickedly 'shed the blood of war in peace,' treacherously, in the gate of a city of refuge.

themes: blood of war shed in peace

schools.summary: David declares himself and his kingdom guiltless before the Lord and turns the guilt on Joab's house; Ellicott affirms the plea was simply true and politically necessary.
Henry: lest any suspect David's connivance, he appeals to God for his innocence — the comfort of being able to say 'we had no hand in it.'
Gill: the delay in the news reaching David is noted to clear the king of all concern in the murder.
The fivefold curse invokes disease, violent death, and poverty on Joab's house; Ellicott shows the deed was no lawful revenge, Hebron being a city of refuge where none may slay without trial.
Clarke reads the curse as prophecy of God's displeasure; Wesley questions whether resolute punishment of the murderer would not have better become David than imprecation on his posterity.
Henry: blood-guilt curses families and God will avenge, yet a firm punishment of Joab himself would have become David better than this passionate imprecation.

debates.question: Was David right to curse Joab's house rather than punish Joab himself?

sides.position: the curse is a just abhorrence and a prophecy of God's judgment
resolute punishment of the murderer would have better become David than imprecation on his posterity

themes: no lawful revenge in a city of refuge

schools.summary: Gill: Abishai shares the guilt as an accessory, consenting and advising, and present when Joab struck.
Ellicott: the Hebrew distinguishes Abner's lawful killing of Asahel in war from the brothers' murder; David's disapproval clears him, but he remains at fault for not punishing Joab.
Wesley: Abishai is guilty by consent and counsel; Asahel's death in self-defence was no justification for this treacherous murder.
Ellicott: the public mourning in rent clothes and sackcloth, with Joab made to do penance and David following the bier, deliberately severs the crown from the crime.
Wesley and Clarke: Joab is made chief mourner to shame him and move him to repentance, and David follows the bier, against royal usage, to clear himself of suspicion.
Gill: that Joab is commanded to mourn rather than seized for justice shows how great his power over David had grown.

themes: the crown cleared of the crime

schools.summary: Ellicott: Abner is buried honourably in the royal city of Hebron rather than his family's Gibeon; Keil reads David's loud weeping, joined by all the people, as clearing him of complicity.
Gill notes the Jewish tradition that Abner was buried within the city, against the usual practice.
Keil: 'the fool' (nabal) is the ungodly (Ps. 14:1); the ode protests that Abner died not a criminal's just death but a treacherous murder.
Wesley: 'as a fool' means 'as a wicked man' — not cut off by justice but by Joab's malice; Clarke calls the short, pathetic elegy a heavy reproof to Joab, sung by all the people.
Henry: David laments both that so wise a man was 'fooled out of his life' by treachery, and that he did not die as a justly condemned felon.

themes: the lament as reproof to Joab

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: Abner's unbound hands and unfettered feet prove he died no condemned malefactor's death but was treacherously murdered while valiant, free, and unsuspecting.
Gill: with hands and feet at liberty Abner could have fought or fled had he known his danger; his freedom made his fall the more treacherous.
Wesley: Abner did not tamely yield nor fall in equal combat, but by the perfidy through which 'the vilest coward may kill the most valiant person.'
Keil and Ellicott: the people urged David to take food, but so bitter was his grief that he refused with an oath till sundown, and his conduct won the people's approval.
Gill: contrary to the custom of the post-burial meal that even kings attended, David refused to eat, binding himself by oath to fast till evening.
Keil: the people perceived David's trouble and were pleased, as all he did pleased them — his sincere grief winning their verdict.
Clarke and Wesley: the people saw David's grief was sincere and that he had no part in Abner's murder, and were satisfied of his integrity.
Henry: the mutual willingness to please between king and people — his care for them, their good opinion of him — makes every relation comfortable.

themes: a godly ruler and his people

schools.summary: Keil: since Joab had fetched Abner back in David's name, suspicion would naturally fall on the king; David's heartfelt grief persuaded all Israel that the murder was not of him.
Gill: not Judah only but all Israel were satisfied by David's words and actions that he had no hand in Abner's death.

themes: murder done in the king's name

schools.summary: Keil: calling Abner 'a prince and a great man' shows David recognised his opponent's virtues, hoped to make him a faithful servant, and would have punished the murderer had he the power.
Henry: David honestly praised Abner's real merit as prince and general without calling him a saint, giving true praise where he could.
Gill: the words were spoken to his courtiers, explaining why the king mourned as he did.
Ellicott and Gill: David's weakness is that of a new, unsettled kingdom; knowing the sons of Zeruiah's lawless power, he dared not punish them and left their judgment to God.
Clarke: Joab had usurped all power, reducing David to a shadow of royalty; Wesley faults the delay as distrust of God and contrary to the law requiring murderers to die.
Henry: David ought to have done his duty and trusted God with the issue; sparing Joab was carnal policy that let further murders follow.

debates.question: Was David's sparing of Joab excusable weakness or culpable fault?

sides.position: real inability — a new king could not yet punish so powerful a subject, so he left it to God
a genuine fault — distrust of God and disobedience to the law requiring murderers to die

themes: the sons of Zeruiah too hard
